text: Robotic parasophageal hernia repair with intraoperative repair of posterior vagus nerve injury. This is a ninety-three-year-old female with history of chronic anemia and a large hiatal hernia. She also complains of intermittent chest pain, heartburn, and regurgitation. She has history of cholecystectomy, appendectomy, and arthrectomy. The upper GI series shows a large parasophageal hernia. Pre-op gastric emptying study was performed, which was normal.

The robotic surgical setup is shown with the patient in a supine position. Trocar placement is depicted, and the patient is positioned in steep reverse Trendelenburg. Adhesions in the midline are lysed, and the stomach is reduced into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon performs initial dissection from the patient's left to right, dividing the short gastric vessels superiorly up to the left crus. The hernia sac is identified and dissected free using blunt and sharp techniques with a bipolar instrument. After identifying and dissecting the left crus, attention shifts to the right crus, continuing dissection from left to right. The anterior vagus nerve is identified, and the esophagus is mobilized for further posterior dissection.

The surgeon identifies both the posterior and anterior vagus nerves. The hernia sac is retracted, and dissection continues with excessive traction from the left side, leading to the unintended dissection of the posterior vagus nerve.

The surgeon identifies the anterior vagus nerve and ensures its preservation. The edges of the injured vagus nerve are cleaned and approximated using two floral PDAs sutures, performed robotically. The hiatus is initially approximated with CRIO V-Lock absorbable suture, followed by interrupted CRIO silk sutures. The hiatal repair is reinforced with an absorbable mesh.

The surgeon performs a partial Toupet fundoplication, fashioning and suturing the fundus with serial sutures. The fundoplication is then anchored to the diaphragm. An intraoperative endoscopy is conducted to assess the surgical repair.
